title: GNSS/GPS and Geodesy
description: GNSS positioning fundamentals and field methods (static, RTK, PPK, OPUS/PPP, RTN), error sources and DOP, the geoid–ellipsoid relationship (H = h − N), datums (NAD83, WGS84, NATRF2022), map projections, the State Plane Coordinate System, grid vs. ground distances via the combined scale factor, convergence, and UTM — all matched to the NCEES FS Reference Handbook v2.5.
key_topics: GNSS constellations (GPS, GLONASS, Galileo, BeiDou) and signal structure
Positioning methods: autonomous, DGPS, static, fast-static, RTK, PPK, OPUS/PPP
Real-time networks (RTN / VRS) and base–rover differential positioning
GNSS error sources: ionosphere, troposphere, multipath, clock, ephemeris
Dilution of precision (DOP) and UERE → position error
Geoid vs. ellipsoid vs. orthometric height: H = h − N
Datums: NAD83, WGS84, NAVD88, GRS80, and the 2022 NATRF/NAPGD reference frames
Map projections: Lambert Conformal Conic vs. Transverse Mercator
State Plane Coordinate System (SPCS) and zone selection
Grid vs. ground: scale factor, elevation factor, combined factor

key_formulas.name: Orthometric / ellipsoidal / geoid height
key_formulas.formula: H = h − N   (equivalently h = H + N)

key_formulas.name: Combined factor
key_formulas.formula: CSF = SF × EF   (scale factor × elevation factor)

content_sections.title: Module Overview
content_sections.content:
## Where geodesy and GNSS sit on the FS

The NCEES FS exam tests geodesy and GNSS under **Surveying Principles** (Area 4: basic surveying, geodesy, and *applied* geodesy — datums, datum conversions, SPCS, map projections, satellite coordinate systems) and under **Surveying Processes and Methods** (Area 1: GNSS/GPS surveys — static, kinematic, OPUS, real-time networks). Together these are roughly an eighth of the exam, and they are conceptually rich rather than calculation-heavy: most points come from *understanding* how a satellite position becomes a grid coordinate on a map.

This module follows the **measurement → reference frame → projection** chain that every modern survey rides:

1. **GNSS measures** a three-dimensional position relative to an **ellipsoid** (a smooth mathematical earth) in a global datum (WGS84 / NAD83 / the 2022 frames).
2. The height it returns is an **ellipsoidal height h**, not the **orthometric height H** (elevation above the **geoid**, the equipotential 'mean sea level' surface). The two are tied by the **geoid undulation N**: `H = h − N`.
3. To put the position on a flat **map** we apply a **projection** — Lambert Conformal Conic for east–west states, Transverse Mercator for north–south states — producing **State Plane** (or UTM) **grid coordinates**.
4. Grid coordinates are not ground distances. The **combined factor** (scale factor × elevation factor) converts between **grid** and **ground**, and **convergence** relates grid north to geodetic north.

Master the vocabulary (geoid vs. ellipsoid, datum vs. projection, grid vs. ground), the one essential height equation **H = h − N**, and the one essential distance chain **grid = ground × CSF**, and you will pick up most of the available points. Every formula here matches the **NCEES FS Reference Handbook v2.5** (Geodesy, State Plane Coordinates, and Conversions sections) so the on-screen reference will look familiar.

content_sections.title: Key Concepts
content_sections.content:
## GNSS fundamentals

**GNSS** (Global Navigation Satellite System) is the umbrella term; **GPS** is the U.S. constellation. The four major constellations are **GPS (USA)**, **GLONASS (Russia)**, **Galileo (EU)**, and **BeiDou (China)**. A receiver computes position by measuring **ranges** to satellites whose orbital positions are known from the broadcast **ephemeris**. Each constellation has three segments: **space** (satellites), **control** (ground monitoring/upload stations), and **user** (receivers).

Two observable types:

- **Code (pseudorange)** — fast to acquire, meter-level; the basis of autonomous and DGPS navigation.
- **Carrier phase** — uses the wavelength of the carrier (~19 cm L1); millimeter-to-centimeter precision once the integer **ambiguity** is resolved. All high-accuracy surveying is carrier-phase.

A single receiver positioning by itself is **autonomous** (a few meters). Accuracy improves through **differencing** against a reference of known coordinates, which cancels common errors.

## Positioning (field) methods

| Method | Observable | Typical accuracy | When used |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Autonomous** | code | 2–5 m | navigation, recon |
| **DGPS / SBAS (WAAS)** | code (differential) | sub-meter to ~1 m | GIS, mapping-grade |
| **Static** | carrier, long occupation (20 min–hours) | mm–cm | control, long baselines |
| **Fast/rapid static** | carrier, short occupation | cm | densifying control |
| **RTK (real-time kinematic)** | carrier, base→rover radio | 1–3 cm | layout, topo, boundary |
| **PPK (post-processed kinematic)** | carrier, processed later | 1–3 cm | when no real-time link |
| **RTN / VRS (network RTK)** | carrier, modeled from a CORS network | 1–3 cm | RTK without your own base |
| **OPUS / PPP** | carrier, processed against CORS / precise orbits | cm | single-receiver static positioning |

**OPUS** (Online Positioning User Service, NGS) post-processes a single static occupation against the **CORS** (Continuously Operating Reference Stations) network and returns published NAD83 and ITRF coordinates. **PPP** (precise point positioning) uses precise satellite orbit/clock products to position a single receiver without a local base.

## GNSS error sources

- **Ionospheric delay** — frequency-dependent; largely removed by **dual-frequency** (L1/L2) receivers; the largest natural error.
- **Tropospheric delay** — modeled (not frequency-dependent); worse at low elevation angles.
- **Satellite clock & ephemeris (orbit) errors** — corrected by broadcast/precise products; differenced out over short baselines.
- **Multipath** — signal reflecting off nearby surfaces; mitigated by choke-ring antennas, siting, and avoiding reflective surfaces. **Not** reduced by differencing (it is site-specific).
- **Receiver noise** and **antenna phase-center variation**.
- **Cycle slips** — loss of carrier lock that must be repaired in processing.

## Dilution of Precision (DOP)

**DOP** quantifies how satellite *geometry* amplifies ranging error into position error. **Lower DOP is better** (well-spread satellites). Position error ≈ **UERE × DOP**, where **UERE** is the user-equivalent range error.

- **GDOP** (geometric, 3D + time), **PDOP** (position, 3D), **HDOP** (horizontal), **VDOP** (vertical), **TDOP** (time).
- VDOP is typically worse than HDOP because all satellites are above the horizon (no satellites 'below' to balance the vertical).

## Heights: geoid, ellipsoid, orthometric — H = h − N

Three surfaces:

- **Ellipsoid** — the smooth mathematical reference (GRS80 for NAD83). GNSS heights are **ellipsoidal heights h** measured along the ellipsoid normal.
- **Geoid** — the equipotential gravity surface approximating mean sea level; the reference for **orthometric height H** (the 'elevation' a level gives).
- **Geoid undulation N** — the separation between geoid and ellipsoid (negative across most of the conterminous U.S.).

**The single most-tested geodesy relationship:**

`H = h − N`   (equivalently `h = H + N`)

A **geoid model** (e.g., GEOID18, and the upcoming **GEOID2022**) supplies N so GNSS ellipsoidal heights can be converted to usable orthometric elevations.

## Datums and reference frames

- **NAD83** — the horizontal datum for U.S. surveying (uses the **GRS80** ellipsoid). Realized through CORS/HARN.
- **WGS84** — the GPS broadcast datum; now essentially coincident with ITRF at the ~cm level (NAD83 differs from WGS84/ITRF by 1–2 m due to plate motion and origin definition).
- **NAVD88** — the current vertical datum (orthometric).
- **GRS80** — the reference ellipsoid (a = 6,378,137 m, 1/f = 298.257222101).
- **2022 modernization (NGS)** — replaces NAD83 and NAVD88 with the **terrestrial reference frames** of the **North American Terrestrial Reference Frame of 2022 (NATRF2022)** (plus Pacific/Caribbean/Mariana frames) for horizontal and the **North American–Pacific Geopotential Datum of 2022 (NAPGD2022)** for vertical, with **GEOID2022** providing N. These are plate-fixed, time-dependent (epoch) frames aligned to the ITRF and realized largely by GNSS/CORS rather than passive marks.

## Map projections and SPCS

A **projection** flattens the curved ellipsoid onto a plane, introducing **distortion**; surveying uses **conformal** projections (angles preserved locally). Two families dominate U.S. State Plane:

- **Lambert Conformal Conic (LCC)** — a cone; best for zones that are **long east–west** (e.g., Texas zones, Pennsylvania).
- **Transverse Mercator (TM)** — a cylinder; best for zones **long north–south** (e.g., Illinois, New Jersey).
- (**Oblique Mercator** is used for the Alaska panhandle Zone 1.)

The **State Plane Coordinate System (SPCS)** divides each state into zones sized so the **scale distortion stays within about 1:10,000**. **UTM** (Universal Transverse Mercator) is a global TM system in 6°-wide zones; its central-meridian scale factor is fixed at **0.9996** (larger distortion than SPCS, so SPCS is preferred for local surveying).

## Grid vs. ground, and convergence

**Grid** distances/coordinates live on the projection; **ground** distances are what you measure with an EDM. Convert with the **combined factor**:

- **Scale factor (SF)** — projection distortion (ellipsoid → grid); near 1 (e.g., 0.9999).
- **Elevation (sea-level) factor (EF)** — reduces a ground distance to the ellipsoid: `EF = R/(R + H + N) ≈ R/(R + h)`, R ≈ 20,906,000 ft.
- **Combined scale factor:** `CSF = SF × EF`.
- **`grid = ground × CSF`**, and **`ground = grid / CSF`**.

**Convergence γ** (the **mapping angle**) is the angle between **grid north** and **geodetic (true) north** at a point; it grows with distance from the zone's central meridian. Grid azimuth = geodetic azimuth − γ + the small **arc-to-chord (t−T)** correction.

content_sections.title: Essential Formulas
content_sections.content:
## Heights (NCEES Handbook — Geodesy)

| Quantity | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Height relationship | `h = H + N`  →  `H = h − N` | h = ellipsoidal, H = orthometric, N = geoid undulation |
| Geoid undulation | `N = h − H` | from a geoid model (GEOID18 / GEOID2022) |

**Memory hook:** ellipsoidal (GNSS) is 'higher up the alphabet' — `h = H + N`. To get the elevation you *subtract* the (usually negative) undulation: `H = h − N`.

## Distance reduction chain (SPCS — Handbook State Plane section)

| Step | Formula |
|---|---|
| Elevation (sea-level) factor | `EF = R / (R + H + N) ≈ R / (R + h)`,  R ≈ 20,906,000 ft |
| Projection scale factor | `SF` (from the zone's projection / published value) |
| Combined scale factor | `CSF = SF × EF` |
| Ground → grid | `D_grid = D_ground × CSF` |
| Grid → ground | `D_ground = D_grid / CSF` |
| Ellipsoid (geodetic) distance | `D_E = D_ground × EF` (ground → ellipsoid) |
| Arc-to-chord (t−T) | `AR − CH = CH³ / (24R²)` |

(For precision looser than ~1:200,000 you may use R ≈ 20,906,000 ft and neglect N, so `EF ≈ R/(R+H)`.)

## GNSS error budget and DOP

| Quantity | Formula |
|---|---|
| Baseline error budget | `σ = a + b·L`  (a in mm, b in ppm, L in km) |
| Position error from DOP | `σ_pos = UERE × DOP`  (use HDOP, VDOP, or PDOP for the matching component) |
| Geometric range, satellite→receiver | `ρ = √((Xs−Xr)² + (Ys−Yr)² + (Zs−Zr)²)` |

## Ellipsoid geometry (Handbook — Geodesy)

| Quantity | Formula |
|---|---|
| Flattening | `f = (a − b)/a`  (published as 1/f) |
| Eccentricity² | `e² = (a² − b²)/a²` |
| Radius in the meridian | `M = a(1 − e²)/(1 − e²·sin²φ)^{3/2}` |
| Radius in the prime vertical | `N_pv = a/(1 − e²·sin²φ)^{1/2}` |
| GRS80 ellipsoid | a = 6,378,137.0 m, 1/f = 298.257222101 |
| Orthometric correction | `−0.005288·sin 2φ·Δφ·(arc 1′)` |

## Convergence / azimuth

`Az_grid = Az_geodetic − γ + (t − T)`

γ = convergence (mapping angle, grid north relative to geodetic north); (t − T) = arc-to-chord correction (small, often negligible for short lines).

content_sections.title: Worked Examples
content_sections.content:
## The measurement-to-map chain, illustrated

<svg width="480" height="260" viewBox="0 0 480 260" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="background:#fff;border:1px solid #ccc">
  <!-- terrain -->
  <path d="M20,70 Q140,40 260,75 T460,70" fill="none" stroke="#7a5230" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="24" y="62" font-size="12" fill="#7a5230">Ground (terrain), height H above geoid</text>
  <!-- geoid -->
  <path d="M20,150 Q140,140 260,152 T460,150" fill="none" stroke="#1c4e80" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="24" y="168" font-size="12" fill="#1c4e80">Geoid (≈ MSL) — datum for orthometric H</text>
  <!-- ellipsoid -->
  <line x1="20" y1="200" x2="460" y2="200" stroke="#888" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="6 4"/>
  <text x="24" y="218" font-size="12" fill="#555">Ellipsoid (GRS80) — datum for ellipsoidal h (GNSS)</text>
  <!-- vertical ties at x=300 -->
  <line x1="300" y1="73" x2="300" y2="200" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1"/>
  <line x1="300" y1="73" x2="300" y2="152" stroke="#c0392b" stroke-width="3"/>
  <text x="305" y="120" font-size="12" fill="#c0392b">H (orthometric)</text>
  <line x1="300" y1="152" x2="300" y2="200" stroke="#1c4e80" stroke-width="3"/>
  <text x="305" y="180" font-size="12" fill="#1c4e80">N (undulation)</text>
  <text x="360" y="140" font-size="12" fill="#333">h = H + N</text>
  <circle cx="300" cy="73" r="3" fill="#000"/>
  <text x="308" y="70" font-size="11">survey point</text>
</svg>

---

## Example 1 — Orthometric height from a GNSS observation (H = h − N)

A GNSS survey gives an **ellipsoidal height h = 1,250.00 ft** at a station. The geoid model returns a **geoid undulation N = −29.50 ft** there. Find the **orthometric height (elevation) H**.

`H = h − N = 1,250.00 − (−29.50) = 1,279.50 ft`

**Why it's larger:** N is negative across most of the lower-48, so the ellipsoid sits *above* the geoid; subtracting a negative undulation *adds* to the elevation. **Calculator (any approved model):** simply `1250 − (−29.5) =`. The only trap is the **sign of N** — read it off the geoid model exactly.

---

## Example 2 — Geoid undulation from known h and H

A benchmark has a published **orthometric elevation H = 612.40 ft** (NAVD88). A static GNSS occupation gives **h = 587.10 ft**. Find the **geoid undulation N** at the mark.

`N = h − H = 587.10 − 612.40 = −25.30 ft`

This is exactly how a GNSS-on-benchmarks campaign *measures* the geoid to validate a model. **Sanity check:** N is negative (typical for the conterminous U.S.), and |N| is a few tens of feet — reasonable.

---

## Example 3 — Combined factor and ground → grid distance

At a project site the **projection scale factor SF = 0.9999012** and the station elevation is **H = 4,500 ft** (geoid undulation small, neglected). Reduce a measured **ground distance of 2,456.78 ft** to a **grid distance** for State Plane.

1. **Elevation factor** `EF = R/(R + H) = 20,906,000 / (20,906,000 + 4,500) = 0.9997848`.
2. **Combined factor** `CSF = SF × EF = 0.9999012 × 0.9997848 = 0.9996860`.
3. **Grid distance** `D_grid = D_ground × CSF = 2,456.78 × 0.9996860 = 2,456.01 ft`.

**Calculator (TI-36X Pro / Casio fx-991):** compute EF, store it (`STO`), multiply by SF for CSF, then multiply the ground distance. The grid distance is **shorter** here because both factors are < 1 (elevation > sea level *and* this zone projects slightly small). **Trap:** at high elevations EF can dominate; near the standard parallels SF can exceed 1.

---

## Example 4 — Grid → ground (the reverse reduction)

State Plane inversed a line as **grid distance 2,456.78 ft**. The combined factor for the area is **CSF = 0.9998765**. What **ground distance** would you stake?

`D_ground = D_grid / CSF = 2,456.78 / 0.9998765 = 2,457.08 ft`

To go from grid back to ground you **divide** by the combined factor (the inverse of grid = ground × CSF). The ground distance is slightly **longer**, by about 0.30 ft over this line — exactly the kind of error that shows up when crews stake grid coordinates without applying the factor.

---

## Example 5 — Baseline error budget and DOP

**(a) Baseline accuracy.** A receiver pair is specified at **a = 5 mm + 1 ppm**. For a **12.34 km** baseline, the expected horizontal uncertainty is:

`σ = a + b·L = 5 mm + (1 ppm)(12.34 km) = 5 + 1×12.34 = 17.34 mm` (≈ 0.017 m).

(1 ppm = 1 mm per km, so b·L = 12.34 mm.)

**(b) Position error from DOP.** If the **UERE ≈ 2.5 m** and the current **PDOP = 3.2**, the expected 3-D position error is:

`σ_pos = UERE × PDOP = 2.5 × 3.2 = 8.0 m`.

**Takeaway:** wait for a **low DOP** window — the same ranging error produces far smaller position error when the satellite geometry is strong. **Calculator:** both are one-line multiplications; the only skill is matching the right DOP (HDOP/VDOP/PDOP) to the component asked.

---

## Example 6 — Convergence and grid azimuth (concept-to-number)

At a station the **convergence (mapping angle) γ = +1°12′30″** and the (t−T) arc-to-chord correction is negligible. A line has a **geodetic azimuth of 87°45′00″**. The **grid azimuth** is:

`Az_grid = Az_geodetic − γ = 87°45′00″ − 1°12′30″ = 86°32′30″`.

Convergence is **positive east of the central meridian** (grid north lies west of true north there), so grid azimuths come out *smaller* than geodetic. **Calculator:** subtract in DMS mode (TI/Casio `°’”`).

content_sections.title: Exam Tips & Calculator Strategy
content_sections.content:
## Strategy for the geodesy / GNSS area

- **This is a vocabulary-and-concept area first, a math area second.** Most points come from knowing *which surface, datum, projection, or method* is correct — fast questions you should bank early in the exam. The few calculations (H = h − N, grid = ground × CSF) are one-liners.
- **Lock down the three surfaces.** Ellipsoid (GNSS h), geoid (orthometric H, ≈ MSL), and the undulation N between them. If you can recite `H = h − N` and explain why N is usually negative in the U.S., you own the most-tested item.
- **Datum vs. projection vs. coordinate system** are different things: a *datum* (NAD83) defines the reference frame; a *projection* (Lambert / Transverse Mercator) flattens it; SPCS/UTM are *coordinate systems* built on a projection. Watch questions that swap these.
- **Know the projection-to-state shape rule:** **Lambert Conformal Conic for east–west zones, Transverse Mercator for north–south zones.** Texas (wide E–W) uses Lambert.
- **Currency:** the FS still references NAD83/NAVD88, but be ready for one item on the **2022 modernization** — NATRF2022 (horizontal), NAPGD2022 (vertical), GEOID2022 — which replaces passive monuments with GNSS/CORS-realized, time-dependent frames.

## Approved-calculator tricks (TI-36X Pro / Casio fx-991 / HP-35S)

- **EF and CSF are short chains — store intermediates.** Compute `EF = R/(R+H)`, `STO` it, multiply by SF for CSF, then by the distance. Re-keying R = 20,906,000 ft each time invites blunders; store it once.
- **Direction of the factor:** **ground → grid = multiply; grid → ground = divide.** Sanity-check: at elevation, ground is longer than grid, so the ground number should be the bigger one.
- **DMS for convergence:** enter γ and azimuths with the `°’”` (TI/Casio) key; HP-35S uses `→HR`/`→HMS`. Keep the calculator in **DEGREE** mode.
- **ppm is mm-per-km:** `b ppm × L km` gives millimeters directly — no unit gymnastics. Add the constant term `a` (also mm).
- **DOP is a pure multiplier:** `σ_pos = UERE × DOP`. Match HDOP to horizontal, VDOP to vertical, PDOP to 3-D.

## Common traps

- **Sign of N:** `H = h − N`. With N negative (typical U.S.), the orthometric height ends up *larger* than the ellipsoidal height. Flip the sign and you lose the point.
- **Multiply vs. divide the combined factor:** grid = ground × CSF; ground = grid ÷ CSF.
- **Lower DOP is better.** A 'high DOP' (poor geometry) inflates position error — don't pick it as the 'good' condition.
- **Multipath is not removed by differencing** (it is site-specific); the dual-frequency cure is for the *ionosphere*.
- **UTM scale factor is fixed 0.9996** at the central meridian; SPCS zones are tighter (~1:10,000), which is *why* SPCS is preferred for local work.
- **Ellipsoidal height ≠ elevation.** A GNSS 'height' is not an orthometric elevation until you apply the geoid model.
- **WGS84 vs. NAD83** differ by 1–2 m — they are not interchangeable for survey-grade work even though both use ~GRS80-class ellipsoids.

## How the handbook helps

The NCEES FS Reference Handbook v2.5 gives, verbatim: the **height relationship h = H + N** and ellipsoid constants (Geodesy section); the **State Plane** distance-reduction chain (`EF = R/(R+H+N)`, combined factor, arc-to-chord); GRS80/Clarke-1866 ellipsoid parameters; and the unit conversions (R ≈ 20,906,000 ft ≈ 6,372,000 m). Know *where* each lives so you can confirm a factor in seconds instead of deriving it under time pressure.

questions.question_text: The relationship among ellipsoidal height (h), orthometric height (H), and geoid undulation (N) is:
questions.explanation: GNSS measures ellipsoidal height h; orthometric height (elevation) H = h − N, where N is the geoid undulation (separation of geoid below/above the ellipsoid). Equivalently h = H + N.

questions.question_text: A GNSS receiver, by itself, directly measures which type of height?
questions.explanation: GNSS positions are referenced to the ellipsoid, so the raw vertical result is an ellipsoidal height. A geoid model is needed to convert it to an orthometric elevation: H = h − N.

questions.question_text: The geoid is best described as:
options: An equipotential gravity surface approximating mean sea level
A smooth mathematical ellipsoid of revolution
The physical topographic surface of the earth
The orbit plane of the GPS satellites
questions.correct_answer: An equipotential gravity surface approximating mean sea level
questions.explanation: The geoid is the equipotential surface of the earth's gravity field that best fits mean sea level; it is everywhere perpendicular to the plumb line. The ellipsoid is the smooth mathematical reference; the two are separated by the undulation N.

questions.question_text: An ellipsoidal height h = 980.00 ft is observed where the geoid undulation N = −27.00 ft. The orthometric elevation H is:
questions.explanation: H = h − N = 980.00 − (−27.00) = 1,007.00 ft. Because N is negative, the orthometric height is larger than the ellipsoidal height.

questions.question_text: Which GNSS error source is largely eliminated by using a dual-frequency (L1/L2) receiver?
options: Receiver clock drift relative to the antenna
questions.explanation: Ionospheric delay is frequency-dependent, so combining two carrier frequencies cancels most of it. Multipath is site-specific (not removed by differencing or dual-frequency); tropospheric delay is not frequency-dependent and is modeled instead.

questions.question_text: Lower values of Dilution of Precision (DOP) indicate:
options: Stronger satellite geometry and a smaller position error
Weaker satellite geometry and a larger position error
A larger number of cycle slips
questions.correct_answer: Stronger satellite geometry and a smaller position error
questions.explanation: DOP scales ranging error into position error: σ_pos ≈ UERE × DOP. Well-distributed satellites give a low DOP and a small position error; clustered satellites give a high DOP.

questions.question_text: With a user-equivalent range error (UERE) of 2.0 m and a PDOP of 4.0, the expected 3-D position error is most nearly:
questions.explanation: σ_pos = UERE × PDOP = 2.0 × 4.0 = 8.0 m. PDOP applies to the 3-D position; HDOP and VDOP would give the horizontal and vertical components.

questions.question_text: The combined scale factor (CSF) used to reduce ground distances to State Plane grid distances is:
questions.explanation: CSF = SF × EF. The scale factor reduces ellipsoid distance to grid; the elevation (sea-level) factor reduces ground distance to the ellipsoid. Their product converts ground directly to grid.

questions.question_text: A ground distance of 1,500.00 ft is to be placed on the State Plane grid where CSF = 0.9999000. The grid distance is most nearly:
questions.explanation: Grid = ground × CSF = 1,500.00 × 0.9999000 = 1,499.85 ft. To go from ground to grid you multiply by the combined factor (here slightly less than 1, so the grid distance is shorter).

questions.question_text: A State Plane grid distance of 2,000.00 ft must be staked on the ground where CSF = 0.9998500. The ground distance is most nearly:
questions.explanation: Ground = grid / CSF = 2,000.00 / 0.9998500 = 2,000.30 ft. To recover the ground distance you divide by the combined factor; the ground value is longer when CSF < 1.

questions.question_text: The elevation (sea-level) factor for reducing a ground distance to the ellipsoid is approximately:
questions.explanation: EF = R/(R + H + N) ≈ R/(R + h), with R ≈ 20,906,000 ft. Higher elevations make EF smaller, shrinking the ground distance toward the ellipsoid.

questions.question_text: The State Plane Coordinate System uses the Lambert Conformal Conic projection for states (or zones) that are elongated:
questions.explanation: Lambert Conformal Conic suits zones long in the east–west direction (e.g., Texas, Pennsylvania). Transverse Mercator is used for zones long north–south (e.g., Illinois).

questions.question_text: The Transverse Mercator projection is best suited to State Plane zones that are elongated:
questions.explanation: Transverse Mercator (a transverse cylinder) keeps distortion low along a north–south band, so it is used for tall, narrow zones. Lambert Conformal Conic handles wide east–west zones.

questions.question_text: State Plane zones are designed so that the maximum scale distortion is kept to about:
questions.explanation: SPCS zones are limited in width so scale distortion stays near 1:10,000. UTM, with wider 6° zones and a 0.9996 central scale factor, allows larger distortion, which is why SPCS is preferred for local surveys.

questions.question_text: The central-meridian scale factor of the Universal Transverse Mercator (UTM) system is:
questions.explanation: UTM fixes the scale factor at 0.9996 along each zone's central meridian (a secant cylinder), spreading distortion across the 6°-wide zone. SPCS zones use values closer to 1 because they are narrower.

questions.question_text: The horizontal datum currently used for U.S. geodetic surveying, based on the GRS80 ellipsoid, is:
questions.explanation: NAD83 (North American Datum of 1983) is the horizontal datum, realized on the GRS80 ellipsoid. NAVD88 is the vertical (orthometric) datum; WGS84 is the GPS broadcast datum; NAD27 is the superseded Clarke-1866 datum.

questions.question_text: Convergence (the mapping angle, γ) on a map projection is the angle between:
options: The ellipsoid normal and the plumb line
questions.explanation: Convergence is the difference between grid north and geodetic north; it is zero on the central meridian and grows with distance east or west of it. The plumb-line/normal difference is the deflection of the vertical (a different quantity).

questions.question_text: OPUS (the NGS Online Positioning User Service) processes a submitted GNSS data set by:
options: Differencing it against the CORS network to return published datum coordinates
Averaging autonomous code positions in real time
Converting the ellipsoid to a Lambert projection
questions.correct_answer: Differencing it against the CORS network to return published datum coordinates
questions.explanation: OPUS post-processes a static occupation against nearby Continuously Operating Reference Stations (CORS) and returns NAD83 and ITRF coordinates for a single receiver, with an accuracy report.

questions.question_text: Real-time kinematic (RTK) surveying achieves centimeter accuracy primarily by:
options: Carrier-phase differencing between a base of known position and a rover
Using a single-frequency receiver with WAAS
Increasing the satellite elevation mask to 45°
questions.correct_answer: Carrier-phase differencing between a base of known position and a rover
questions.explanation: RTK resolves carrier-phase ambiguities and differences the rover against a base (or network) of known coordinates in real time, canceling correlated errors and yielding 1–3 cm positions.

questions.question_text: A real-time network (RTN) or VRS solution lets a surveyor perform RTK without:
options: Setting up their own base station on a known point
Any cellular or radio data link
questions.correct_answer: Setting up their own base station on a known point
questions.explanation: An RTN models corrections from a network of CORS and delivers them (often as a virtual reference station) to the rover, so the surveyor needs only one receiver and a data link — no local base.

questions.question_text: Which best describes multipath error in GNSS?
options: Signal reflecting off nearby surfaces before reaching the antenna
Drift of the satellite atomic clocks
questions.correct_answer: Signal reflecting off nearby surfaces before reaching the antenna
questions.explanation: Multipath is reflected signal arriving along with the direct signal, biasing the range. It is site-specific and is mitigated by antenna design (choke ring) and good siting, not by differencing.

questions.question_text: The NGS 2022 datum modernization replaces NAD83 and NAVD88 with new reference frames realized primarily through:
options: GNSS and the CORS network (time-dependent terrestrial reference frames)
A new nationwide network of passive brass monuments
questions.correct_answer: GNSS and the CORS network (time-dependent terrestrial reference frames)
questions.explanation: The 2022 frames (e.g., NATRF2022 horizontal, NAPGD2022 vertical with GEOID2022) are plate-fixed, epoch-dependent and realized by GNSS/CORS rather than passive marks — a shift away from monument-based datums.

questions.question_text: For a GNSS baseline specified as 5 mm + 1 ppm, the expected error on a 20 km baseline is most nearly:
questions.explanation: σ = a + b·L = 5 mm + (1 ppm)(20 km) = 5 + 20 = 25 mm. One ppm equals 1 mm per km, so the distance-dependent term is 20 mm.

questions.question_text: True or False: WGS84 and NAD83 are identical datums and their coordinates can be used interchangeably for survey-grade work.
questions.explanation: False. WGS84 (GPS broadcast / essentially ITRF) and NAD83 differ by 1–2 m because of plate motion and origin definition. They must not be treated as interchangeable at survey accuracy.

questions.question_text: At an elevation of 6,000 ft (geoid undulation neglected), the elevation factor EF = R/(R+H), with R = 20,906,000 ft, is most nearly:
questions.explanation: EF = 20,906,000 / (20,906,000 + 6,000) = 20,906,000 / 20,912,000 = 0.999713. Higher ground means a smaller EF, shrinking ground distances toward the ellipsoid.

questions.question_text: True or False: To convert a State Plane grid distance to a ground distance, you divide the grid distance by the combined scale factor.
questions.explanation: True. Grid = ground × CSF, so ground = grid ÷ CSF. When CSF < 1 the ground distance comes out longer than the grid distance.

questions.question_text: The reference ellipsoid associated with NAD83 is:
questions.explanation: NAD83 uses the GRS80 ellipsoid (a = 6,378,137 m, 1/f = 298.257222101). Clarke 1866 underlies the older NAD27; WGS84 uses an ellipsoid essentially identical to GRS80.

problem_templates.name: Orthometric height from GNSS (H = h − N)
problem_templates.question_template: A GNSS survey returns an ellipsoidal height h = {{h:f2}} ft at a station. The geoid model gives a geoid undulation N = {{N:f2}} ft. Compute the orthometric height (elevation) H, in feet.
problem_templates.explanation_template: The orthometric height is H = h − N = {{h:f2}} − ({{N:f2}}) = {{_answer}} ft. Because the geoid undulation N is negative, subtracting it makes H larger than the ellipsoidal height h.

problem_templates.name: Geoid undulation from h and H (N = h − H)
problem_templates.question_template: A benchmark has a published orthometric elevation H = {{H:f2}} ft. A static GNSS occupation gives an ellipsoidal height h = {{h:f2}} ft. Compute the geoid undulation N at the mark, in feet.
problem_templates.explanation_template: From h = H + N, the geoid undulation is N = h − H = {{h:f2}} − {{H:f2}} = {{_answer}} ft. A negative N (typical of the conterminous U.S.) means the geoid lies below the ellipsoid here.

problem_templates.name: Elevation factor at a station
problem_templates.question_template: A station is at elevation H = {{H:f2}} ft (geoid undulation neglected). Using a mean earth radius R = 20,906,000 ft, compute the elevation (sea-level) factor EF = R/(R + H). Report to 6 decimal places.
problem_templates.explanation_template: EF = R/(R + H) = 20,906,000 / (20,906,000 + {{H:f2}}) = {{_answer}}. A higher elevation gives a smaller EF, reducing the ground distance toward the ellipsoid.

problem_templates.name: Combined factor (scale × elevation)
problem_templates.question_template: At a project site the projection scale factor SF = {{SF:f7}} and the station elevation is H = {{H:f2}} ft (geoid undulation neglected, R = 20,906,000 ft). Compute the combined scale factor CSF. Report to 7 decimal places.
problem_templates.explanation_template: First EF = R/(R + H) = {{EF:f7}}. Then CSF = SF × EF = {{SF:f7}} × {{EF:f7}} = {{_answer}}. Multiply a ground distance by this combined factor to obtain the State Plane grid distance.

problem_templates.name: Ground distance reduced to grid
problem_templates.question_template: A ground distance of {{dist:f2}} ft is to be placed on the State Plane grid where the combined scale factor CSF = {{csf:f7}}. Compute the grid distance, in feet.
problem_templates.explanation_template: To go from ground to grid, multiply by the combined factor: D_grid = {{dist:f2}} × {{csf:f7}} = {{_answer}} ft. With CSF < 1 the grid distance is shorter than the measured ground distance.

problem_templates.name: Grid distance reduced to ground
problem_templates.question_template: A State Plane inverse gives a grid distance of {{dist:f2}} ft. The combined scale factor for the area is CSF = {{csf:f7}}. Compute the ground distance to stake, in feet.
problem_templates.explanation_template: To recover the ground distance, divide the grid distance by the combined factor: D_ground = {{dist:f2}} / {{csf:f7}} = {{_answer}} ft. With CSF < 1 the ground distance is longer than the grid distance.

problem_templates.name: GNSS baseline error budget (a + b·ppm)
problem_templates.question_template: A GNSS receiver pair is specified at {{a:f1}} mm + {{b:f1}} ppm. For a baseline of {{km:f2}} km, compute the expected horizontal uncertainty, in millimeters.
problem_templates.explanation_template: The baseline error is σ = a + b·L = {{a:f1}} mm + {{b:f1}} ppm × {{km:f2}} km = {{_answer}} mm. One ppm equals 1 mm per km, so the distance-dependent term is b·L millimeters.

problem_templates.name: Position error from DOP (σ = UERE × DOP)
problem_templates.question_template: A GNSS observation has a user-equivalent range error UERE = {{uere:f2}} m and a PDOP of {{pdop:f2}}. Compute the expected 3-D position error, in meters.
problem_templates.explanation_template: Position error scales the ranging error by the geometry: σ_pos = UERE × PDOP = {{uere:f2}} × {{pdop:f2}} = {{_answer}} m. A lower DOP (stronger geometry) gives a smaller position error.

mock_questions.question_text: GNSS measures ellipsoidal height h. To obtain the orthometric elevation H needed for engineering, the surveyor applies the geoid undulation N using:
mock_questions.explanation: Orthometric height H = h − N, where N comes from a geoid model. With N negative across most of the U.S., H is larger than h.

mock_questions.question_text: Which surface is the reference for orthometric heights (elevations) obtained by spirit leveling?
mock_questions.explanation: Orthometric heights are referenced to the geoid (the equipotential 'mean sea level' surface). GNSS heights are referenced to the ellipsoid; the two differ by the undulation N.

mock_questions.question_text: A ground distance of 3,200.00 ft is reduced to grid with a combined factor of 0.9999200. The grid distance is most nearly:
mock_questions.explanation: Grid = ground × CSF = 3,200.00 × 0.9999200 = 3,199.74 ft. Multiply ground by the combined factor to get grid (shorter, since CSF < 1).

mock_questions.question_text: A State Plane grid distance of 1,000.00 ft is to be staked where CSF = 0.9998000. The ground distance is most nearly:
mock_questions.explanation: Ground = grid / CSF = 1,000.00 / 0.9998000 = 1,000.20 ft. Divide grid by the combined factor to recover the longer ground distance.

mock_questions.question_text: For which State Plane zone shape is the Lambert Conformal Conic projection appropriate?
mock_questions.explanation: Lambert Conformal Conic minimizes distortion across east–west bands; Transverse Mercator is used for north–south bands.

mock_questions.question_text: The Universal Transverse Mercator system applies a scale factor along the central meridian of each zone equal to:
mock_questions.explanation: UTM uses 0.9996 at the central meridian of each 6° zone, distributing distortion across the zone. SPCS zones keep distortion nearer 1:10,000 by being narrower.

mock_questions.question_text: Which GNSS error is reduced by combining two carrier frequencies (L1/L2)?
mock_questions.explanation: Ionospheric delay is frequency-dependent and is largely canceled by a dual-frequency solution. Tropospheric delay is modeled; multipath is mitigated by siting and antenna design.

mock_questions.question_text: A surveyor wants the smallest GNSS position error. The most favorable condition is:
options: A short observation with few satellites
mock_questions.explanation: σ_pos ≈ UERE × DOP, so a low DOP from good satellite geometry minimizes position error. High DOP means poor geometry and larger error.

mock_questions.question_text: NGS's OPUS service is best described as a tool to:
options: Post-process a single static GNSS occupation against the CORS network
Provide real-time RTK corrections in the field
Draft a Lambert projection from field data
mock_questions.correct_answer: Post-process a single static GNSS occupation against the CORS network
mock_questions.explanation: OPUS returns published NAD83/ITRF coordinates for one receiver by differencing the data against nearby CORS — an office (post-processing) tool, not a real-time service.

mock_questions.question_text: The horizontal datum currently in use for U.S. geodetic control, built on GRS80, is:
mock_questions.explanation: NAD83 on the GRS80 ellipsoid is the U.S. horizontal datum. NAVD88 is the vertical datum; NAD27 (Clarke 1866) is superseded.

mock_questions.question_text: Convergence (the mapping angle) at a State Plane station is:
options: Zero on the central meridian and increases away from it
mock_questions.correct_answer: Zero on the central meridian and increases away from it
mock_questions.explanation: Convergence — the angle between grid north and geodetic north — is zero on the central meridian and grows with east–west distance from it. It is unrelated to magnetic declination.

mock_questions.question_text: The 2022 NGS datum modernization (NATRF2022 / NAPGD2022) differs from NAD83/NAVD88 chiefly in that the new frames are:
options: Time-dependent and realized by GNSS/CORS rather than passive monuments
Defined only for the state of Texas
mock_questions.correct_answer: Time-dependent and realized by GNSS/CORS rather than passive monuments
mock_questions.explanation: The 2022 frames are plate-fixed, epoch-dependent and realized through GNSS and the CORS network, with GEOID2022 supplying N — a move away from passive monument-based datums.

mock_questions.question_text: A GNSS baseline processor reports 5 mm + 1 ppm accuracy. Over a 15 km baseline the expected uncertainty is most nearly:
mock_questions.explanation: σ = a + b·L = 5 mm + (1 ppm)(15 km) = 5 + 15 = 20 mm (1 ppm = 1 mm per km).

mock_questions.question_text: RTK and network-RTK (RTN/VRS) surveys reach centimeter accuracy by:
options: Carrier-phase differencing against a base or modeled network
Averaging thousands of autonomous code positions
Using only the L1 C/A code
Raising the elevation mask to exclude all low satellites
mock_questions.correct_answer: Carrier-phase differencing against a base or modeled network
mock_questions.explanation: Both resolve carrier-phase ambiguities and difference the rover against a known base (RTK) or a modeled network/VRS (RTN), canceling correlated errors for 1–3 cm results.
